Ganesh "Babarao" Savarkar

[Veer Savarkar](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/veer-savarkar/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Ganesh "Babarao" Savarkar

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jun 13, 2025

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

Share

While we are familiar with the life and times of Veer Savarkar, not much is known about his elder brother Ganesh Savarkar, affectionately called as Babarao, a true nationalist, who suffered and sacrificed as much, in fact more.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SSxc!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F94b1dbc4-f719-4b0e-8398-c3f85d395688_269x187.jpeg)

![Remembering Ganesh 'Babarao' Savarkar ...](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SSxc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F94b1dbc4-f719-4b0e-8398-c3f85d395688_269x187.jpeg "Remembering Ganesh 'Babarao' Savarkar ...")

Babarao was born on June 13, 1879 in Bhagur near to Nashik, the eldest of the 3 brothers, followed by Vinayak Savarkar and Narayan Savarkar. It was a hard life for him from beginning, with mother Radhabai passing away when he was just 13, and their father Damodar Pant, passing away in a plague epidemic couple of years later. And he had to shoulder the family responsiblity, as well as look after his brothers at a much younger age. He gave up his own education due to the financial responsiblities, due to which his role in the revolutionary movement was initially limited.

However he began to actively take part in the revolutionary movement later and in 1906 he took up the responsibility of Abhinav Bharat, the organization founded by his younger brother Vinayak, when the latter left for London to pursue higher education. He regularly invited thinkers like Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Aurobindo Ghosh, S M Paranjpe to deliver lectures, and organized processions on Ganesh Chaturthi, Shivaji Jayanti, Dussehra where the slogans of "Chattrapathi Shivaji Maharaj ki Jai” were raised.

During one such procession, when slogans of Vande Mataram were being raised, the police objected, following which a scuffle broke out, where Babarao thrashed one of the officers, after he was struck on the head. He was arrested along with other revolutionaries and put on trial before W.Plunkett, the 1st Class Magistrate of Malegaon division. The trial that went on for a year would become famous as the Vande Mataram trial.

Tilak in the meanwhile was arrested in June, 1908 for writing an article Deschace Durdhaiva( Country’s misfortune) and placed in the same Dongri Jail, where Babarao was serving his sentence. It was then he met Tilak and asked him

*“Now that you are arrested, what would happen to Maharashtra”*

Tilak replied back

*“Baba, don’t worry about Maharashtra, if it is alive it will not die with one man less, if it’s dead, one man alone can’t bring it to life”.*

It was the only time they ever met, with Babarao shifted to Thane, and Tilak deported to Mandaly for a 6 year sentence.

Even after release Babarao was constantly tracked by the police, along with Pandurang Bapat who had returned from London, and taught him the techniques of bomb making. Babarao was constantly on the move now, shifting from area to another, to avoid the police, and he also began to teach younger revolutionaries on the techniques of bomb making.

He was finally caught on February, 1909, when he had gone to receive an arms consignment sent by Tatya from London. Taken to the Nashik jail, he was asked by an officer scornfully

*“So you intend to overthrow the British, how do you plan to go about it”*

To which he shot back

*“The British have promised to grant us freedom, when we become worthy of it, so we plan to make ourselves, worthy by intensifying our desire for freedom.”*

Charged under sections 121, and 124A of IPC, for treason, Babarao was sentenced to life on June 8, 1909 at the notorious Cellular Jail in Andamans. All his properties were to be confiscated, and in order to make sure no one else in Nashik would dare protest against the British, he was paraded in chains, through the streets, and made to wear a yellow cap, that marked his deportation to Kalapani.

So much was the fear that none of the Nashik residents, turned up to see Babarao when he was being taken away. The news of Babarao’s arrest reached India House in London, where Tatya was staying with other revolutionaries. And on July 1, 1909, Madanlal Dhingra shot dead Sir Curzon Wylie in protest, against the deportation. A very close associate of Tatya, he was hanged later in August.

Another group consisting of Krishnaji Karve, Anant Kanhere and Vinayak Deshpande, plotted the assasination of Arthur Jackson, the DM of Nashik, who was instrumental in Babarao’s deportation. The trio shot dead Jackson at Vijayanand Theater, Nashik on December 21, 1909, and were later hanged to death.

Babarao was transported to Cellular Jail in 1910, where the prisoners were put to the worst form of punishment and torture. He was yoked to the kolhu, and made to pull it along in place of oxen, all day long, also had to stand all day with handcuffs. Most of the guards were Muslims who would regularly beat up the prisoners, try to forcibly convert them to Islam.

Prisoners were placed in solitary confinement, and not allowed to talk to each other. So much that Tatya who had arrived in Cellular Jail in 1911, could only interact with Babarao after two long years. Both the brothers together raised a series of protests against the torture and harassment of prisoners there.

By the end of 1919, Babarao’s health deteriorated in prison, owing to the torture he had been put through, lost weight and was diagnosed with TB. Finally in 1921, after sustained pressure from other leaders, and outrage over the conditions in Cellular Jail, the Savarkar brothers were transferred to another prison in India.

Initially placed in Alipore Jail, Babarao was later transferred to Bijapur where he was kept in solitary confinement for eight months. He was later shifted to Sabarmati in January 1922, where his health deteriorated even further, that the authorities believed he would die soon. However due to efforts of his younger brother Narayan and advocate Jairamdas Daulatram, and apprehensive of a public backlash, he was finally released in September 1922.

13 long years in prison, under some of the most terrible conditions, that had broken him down physically and mentally, he was finally able to walk free. On his release, he began to question the Gandhian ideas of non violence, which he felt were not practical. He also believed that constant self loathing would not win the trust of the British, and was against Gandhi’s romantic idealism of Hindu- Muslim unity, borne out of his bitter experiences in prison.

He regularly took part in the Hindu Mahasabha activities to awaken the Hindu consciousness, and founded the Tarun Hindu Sabha in 1923-24 for the youth. He travelled all over, establishing branches, enlisting able bodied Hindus in the age group of 16-40 into the fold. They would participate in shudhi and act against caste discrimination.

When KB Hegdewar founded RSS in 1925 on occasion of Vijaya Dashami, Babarao decided to merge his Tarun Hindu Sabha with it. Dr. Hegdewar who had great regards for Babarao, would often consult him on various issues, and also asked him to draft the pledge for RSS. Later Babarao would often accompany Hegdewar and played an important role in expansion of the Sangh.

In spite of his limited education, Babarao studied Ayurveda, Yoga, Jyotish on his own. He also wrote a Marathi book “Rashtramimansa va Hindustanche Rashtraswaroop”.

Babarao and Tatya had totally contrasting personalities, where Tatya was an atheist, and had a more utilitarian outlook, Babarao was more religious. However both the brothers had a mutual respect and love for each other. Babarao had lost everything in life, his property was confiscated, his children died in their infancy, his wife Yesubai was thrown out of her home, and literally on the streets when arrested. She passed away when he was in Cellular Jail. Yet in spite of all the trials and tribulations, he never wavered even once in his beliefs.

In his last days, at Sangli when he was ailing, Tatya, stood by his bedside and told him

*“Baba, we brothers had a common life mission, we have repaid our ancestors in our generations”.*

And finally on March 16, 1945 he passed away, a long life spent in the fight for the freedom of his mother land and the Hindu cause. A hero who deserves to be known and appreciated as much as his more famous brother.
